# Welcome to the ISoP Agentic Workflows Group — Otter AI Summary

**Date:** June 25, 2026
**Source:** Otter.ai auto-generated summary

> ⚠️ **Disclaimer:** Unfortunately the AI note-taker only captured the first ~30 minutes of the meeting (out of the full ~1 hour). The summary below reflects only what was captured. For a complete account, please refer to the official summary and slides.

---

## Overview

The ISoP Agentic Workflows Group meeting, co-chaired by Ari PB and Peter Bloomingdale, saw a strong turnout with nearly 20 participants. The group aims to develop and standardize Agentic Workflows in pharmacometrics. Key points included the 50/50 split between those learning and building, with 75% using some form of CLI agent or custom workflows. The meeting covered the group's mission, the importance of trust in AI systems, and the division into three working lanes: community and shared learning, discovery and harmonization of tools, and evaluation and trust. Participants shared their backgrounds and interests, highlighting a diverse range of expertise and experience.

---

## Outline



### Introduction and Meeting Purpose
- Ari PB introduces himself and mentions co-chairing the group with Peter Bloomingdale.
- The group is named the Working Group on Agentic Workflows and Pharmacometrics.
- The meeting aims to set the foundation and discuss the group's organization.

### Mission and Participation
- Ari PB reads the group's mission to bring together a community of pharmacometricians.
- The mission includes discovering, evaluating, and producing Agentic Workflows.
- Ari PB shares feedback from the intake form, noting a 50/50 split between those learning and those building.
- The group is divided into three working lanes: community and shared learning, discovery and harmonization of tools, and evaluation and trust.
- Ari PB emphasizes the importance of learning by doing and the need for community input.

### Raw Data and Deliverables
- Ari PB presents raw data on how participants want to participate and their levels of experience.
- The data shows a 50/50 split between technical contribution and learning and networking.
- Most participants are using some form of CLI agent or custom workflows.
- The group's deliverables include a roadmap, glossaries, and learning materials.
- The focus is on building a community of practice and creating verifiable standards.

### Introductions and Backgrounds
- Ari PB and Peter Bloomingdale introduce themselves and their backgrounds.
- Amruta Bhat introduces herself as a PhD student at UB University, focusing on pharmacometrics and clinical pharmacology.
- Brian Corrigan shares his background at Pfizer and his interest in standards and benchmarks.
- Euibeom Shin introduces himself as a PharmD student at the University at Buffalo, interested in AI and pharmacometrics.
- Galina Bernstein introduces herself as a clinical pharmacologist and consultant, interested in AI and machine learning in pharmaceutical research.

- Jagdeep Podichetty introduces himself as the Senior Director of Predictive Analytics and AI at Critical Path.

- Phil Bowsher introduces himself as the head of pharma at Posit, interested in AI in PK and drug development.

### Final Introductions and Closing Remarks
- Sam Callisto introduces himself as a pharmacometrician at Metro Research Group, interested in AI tooling.
- Se Jin Kim introduces himself as a pharmacometrician at Lilly, interested in reproducibility and deterministic outputs.
- Venelin Mitov introduces himself as an independent PKPD consultant and software developer, interested in Agentic Workflows.
- Wenzhe Ma introduces himself as a physicist in the pharma industry, interested in AI in drug design.
- Wu Xiaoxuan introduces himself as a master student in pharmacometrics, interested in AI and pharmacometrics.
- Jeff Sachs introduces himself as a professional mathematician and AI expert at Merck, interested in learning more about AI in pharmacometrics.
